INDIAN NAVY.

That nominees shall not be under 15 years or above 18 years of age, unless they shall have served on board a steam-vessel or under an engineer in a factory or foundry from the completion of their 18th year up to the time of their being put in nomination; and that in such case the nominees shall not exceed 19 years.

That no person who has been dismissed the army or navy, or who has been obliged to quit any school or institution for immoral or ungentlemanly conduct, will be appointed to the Indian navy.

That volunteers for the Indian navy be required to proceed to India within three months after their appointment shall be completed, or their appointment will be considered as forfeited.

That nominees shall produce a certificate that they have undergone an examination in arithmetic and the elementary branches of nautical education, and that they are qualified to enter upon the service.

That nominees shall produce a certificate from a respectable engineer that they have acquired such a knowledge of marine engineering as to afford promise of efficient service on board a steam-vessel.

FURLOUGH.

A certain proportion of the officers (to be determined by the Government, with a due regard to the exigencies of the service)

are allowed to come home on furlough for three years, with the pay only of their rank.

No officer under the rank of captain who has not actually served ten years can be permitted to come home on furlough, unless in cases of ill-health, under the like certificates as required from military officers.

ALLOWANCES.

Captains .....a year £360

Commanders..... 270

Lieutenants and Purkers ..... 165

Midshipmen and Clerks..... 75

RETIREMENT.

Every officer who has actually served twenty-two years or upwards in India is permitted to retire with the following pay:

Captains .....a year £360

Commanders..... 290

Lieutenants ..... 190

Purkers ..... 190

Every officer retiring, from ill-health, after ten years' service, and before they have completed that of twenty-two years, is granted the following retiring allowance:

Captains .....a year £200

Commanders..... 170

Lieutenants ..... 125

Purkers ..... 125

NEW NAVY FURLOUGH REGULATIONS.—Nov. 1, 1855.FURLOUGH ON PRIVATE AFFAIRS.

Every officer will be allowed a furlough to Europe, or elsewhere beyond sea, for a period of two years, at the expiration of ten years' service, and for a second period of two years after twenty years' service in India.

The second furlough of two years will only be granted after ten years' service in India, from the date of the return to duty after the first furlough of two years. These furloughs are not to be cumulative under any circumstances, but are limited to two years. Officers who have taken leave of absence on sick certificate to Europe, or

elsewhere beyond sea, will be ineligible to a furlough on private affairs until they have served six years from their last return to duty. This rule does not apply to short leaves to sea.

All grants of furloughs on private affairs are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and to the regulations framed for the preservation of the efficiency of the Indian navy, by limiting the number of absences.

Officers holding detached or shore appointments may be allowed furlough for a period of six months without losing their appointment; but during that period they will not be entitled to any portion of their salary,

NEW NAVY FURLOUGH REGULATIONS. xxix

which will be drawn in full by the officers officiating for them.

The Europe furlough pay to be granted in the above cases is as follows:—

a day.
£. s. d.
To Capt. the pay of Lieut.— } 1 0 0
Col. in army ..... } 1 0 0
To Commanders, as Majors ... } 0 16 0
To Lieutenants and Purser, as} } 0 10 6
Captains ..... } 0 6 6
To Mates above 3 years' service} } 0 6 6
as such, as Lieuts. .... } 0 5 3
To Clerks, as Ensigns ..... } 0 5 3

LEAVE OF ABSENCE ON SICK CERTIFICATE.

Leave of absence on sick certificate will be granted for a term not exceeding eighteen months, whether it be to any place in Europe or elsewhere out of India.

Extensions of such leave may be granted, as at present, on production of medical certificates, for further periods not exceeding eighteen months in the whole.

An officer holding a detached appointment will be allowed to be absent on sick certificate in England, or elsewhere beyond sea, for a period of fifteen months, without losing his appointment, and during the first six months only of such absence he will be entitled to draw the Indian unemployed shore pay of his rank, and half the difference between that and the salary of his appointment, the officer officiating for him drawing the unemployed shore pay of his rank, and the remaining half salary during the first six months, and the full salary of the appointment during the remainder of the time.

An officer's claim to half the salary for six months, as sanctioned in the preceding rule, will not be affected by his subsequently retiring from the service in Europe, or elsewhere.

No portion of command allowance, medical staff salary, or other similar allowance, which under the old rules was inadmissible to officers of the Indian navy, absent from their ships at the Cape or elsewhere, will be allowed to absent officers under these regulations.

All officers on detached or shore employ, while on leave of absence on sick certificate, will be entitled for a period of six months to pay as above stated, and for the remaining period of their leave they will receive furlough pay only.

If the leave on sick certificate be extended, furlough pay will be continued; but under no circumstances for a longer period than three years altogether from the commencement of the absence.

Officers on detached or shore employ, absent on leave beyond the periods stated in paragraphs 4 and 8, cannot retain their appointments and allowances, except on

special grounds, to be sanctioned by the Government of the Presidency; any such special extension of leave is to be reported at once to the Secretary of State for India in Council, with the reason for it, and to be subject to his approbation.

The privilege of retaining appointments during leave of absence on sick certificate, or on furlough on private affairs, will not be available a second time if the fresh leave is on sick certificate, until an officer has been three years, or, if on private affairs, until he has been six years, in the discharge of the duties of his appointment from the date of last return to it.

This rule, however, does not apply to leaves of absence, whether on sick certificate or on private affairs, in India, or for brief periods to Ceylon, the Mauritius, or other places out of India.

DATE OF FURLOUGH AND ADVANCE OF PAY.

The period of furlough on private affairs, or leave of absence on sick certificate, is to be calculated from the date of the departure from India of the ship in which the officer proceeds.

Advances of pay and allowances to officers proceeding on leave of absence on sick certificates who may be entitled to them, will be made in India for three months from the date of embarkation. Payments in continuation will be made in England at the rates of exchange fixed annually for repayment of advances made on account of Her Majesty's Government. Furlough pay will continue to be issued in advance for six months when required by officers obtaining furlough on private affairs.

The existing regulations regarding passage-money to officers are not affected by these rules.

The certificates issued to officers who are granted furloughs on private affairs, or leave of absence on sick certificate, are to specify the time each officer has been absent on leave within Indian limits, as well as the time of his previous absence in Europe.

The last pay certificate of all officers proceeding to England on sick certificate is to specify the rate of pay and allowances to which they may be entitled.

This information is necessary, in order that no difficulty may arise in the Pay Department of the India Office in adjusting the several payments which may be due.

PERIODS OF ABSENCE TO BE COUNTED AS SERVICE FOR RETIRING PENSIONS.

Officers to be entitled to retire on the full pay of their rank (under the regulations of 1796) must have served twenty-two years in India, out of which leaves of absence for two years in the aggregate, and not more,

will be allowed to count as service for the retiring pension.

All officers now in the service, who prefer retaining what may be considered the advantages of the old regulations, may do so, provided they signify their wish to that effect when they next apply for leave of absence, without any limitation, whether such leave be in India or elsewhere, and officers who once make their election will not be entitled to any other choice, but will be afterwards subject to the new or old regulations, according to their decisions.

Officers holding shore appointments have not the above option allowed them, but they may retain the advantages of the old regulations by giving up their appointments, which in such cases will be considered vacated from the date of embarkation.

To those officers who adopt them, the new regulations will apply retrospectively in respect of furlough and leave of absence beyond sea, and in computing their time of service for retirement, so that their furloughs or leaves of absence, whether under the old or new rules, and whether on private affairs or sick certificate, within or beyond India, will count for service to the authorized extent only, in computing the time for retirement on full pay.

These rules do not in any way affect the existing regulations regarding the grant of leave in India, or for short periods to sea, whether on private affairs or on sick certificate, except as to the periods of such absence being taken into calculation in the time allowed to count as service for retirement on full pay.

ALLOWANCES.

OFFICERS SERVING AFLOAT.

Officers of the Indian Navy are to be allowed, during their absence on sick leave in England, pay as follows:—

Captains.—The shore pay of captain, Rs. 400 per mensem, being Indian shore or unemployed pay, for the first six months, and afterwards furlough pay at the rate of £1 per diem.

Commanders.—Rs. 300 per mensem, being Indian or unemployed shore pay, for the first six months, and afterwards furlough pay, or 16s. per diem.

Senior Lieutenants.—Indian pay, Rs.

191-8-1 per mensem for the first six months, and 10s. 6d. per diem for the remainder of their furlough.

Junior Lieutenants and Purser.—Rs. 161-8-1 for the first six months, and 10s. 6d. per diem for the remainder of their furlough.

Mates above three years' service as such, 6s. 6d. per diem.

Mates under three years' service, and Clerks after six years' service, 5s. 3d. per diem.

And, Midshipmen and Clerks under six years' service, £75 per annum during the currency of their furlough.

BENGAL PILOT SERVICE.

That no person be appointed who is under 15 or past 17 years of age, unless he has already been at sea; in which case, provided his age do not exceed 19 years, he shall be eligible upon producing a certificate of having served at sea for as many years or months as his age shall at the time of appointment exceed 17 years.

That volunteers for the Bengal Pilot Service be required to proceed to India within three months after their appointment shall be completed, or such appointment will be considered as forfeited.

First Mates ..... 50 do. 60 do.
Second Mates and Volunteers ..... 40 do. 30 do.

Mem.—Military and Marine (Furlough and Retired) Pay made up to the 31st January, 30th April, 31st July, and 31st October in each year, and issued on or after the second Monday in February, May, August, and November.

LORD CLIVE'S FUND.REGULATIONS FOR THE ADMISSION OF PENSIONERS.

Every petitioning officer and soldier must produce a certificate from his commanding officer of his being an invalid, and rendered incapable of further service in India, together with an approbation of such certificate by the Governor and Council of the Presidency where he shall have served.

Every commissioned officer must previously make oath before the governor and council, viz.: a colonel, that he is not possessed of, or entitled to, real and personal property to the value of £4000; a lieut.-colonel, £3000; a major, £2500; a captain, £2000; a lieutenant, £1000; an ensign, £750. Officers' widows must produce proof, on affidavit, that their husbands did not die possessed of property as above.

Petitioners residing in England may be admitted if adjudged to be proper objects.

All commissioned, staff, or warrant officers, to have half the ordinary pay they enjoyed whilst in service, viz.—

a year. a day.
Colonels and members Med. Board ..... £228 2 6 or 12s. 6d.
Lieut.-cols. and sup. surgeons ..... 182 10 0 " 10 0
Majors and chaplains (15 years' service) ..... 136 17 6 " 7 6
Captains and surgeons, chaplains (7 years' service),
and vet. surgeons (20 years' service) .....		 91 5 0 " 5 0
Chaplains under 7 years' service ..... 63 17 6 " 3 6
Lieutenants, assistant surgeons, and veterinary sur-
geons under 20 years .....		 45 12 6 " 2 6
Ensigns ..... 36 10 0 " 2 0
Conductors of ordnance ..... 36 10 0 " 2 0

Their widows one-half the above, to continue during their widowhood.

Sergeants of artillery to have ninepence per day, and those that have lost a limb one shilling per day. Gunners of the artillery sixpence per day, and those that have lost a limb ninepence per day.

All other non-commissioned officers and bombardiers to have fourpence three farthings per day.

Officers and privates to be entitled from the period of their landing in England.

Pensioners neglecting to claim the pension for three half-years will be considered as dead and no arrears for a longer period than two years back from the date of application for admission or re-admission, as the case may be, will be allowed either to claimants or to pensioners after admission.

N.B.—Officers admitted to the benefit of the Retiring Regulations are not entitled to participate in Lord Clive's Fund.

COMMISSIONERS OF REVENUE AND CIRCUIT FOR DISTRICTS.

Agra, Muttra, Furruckabad, Mynpoorie, Etawah—G. F. Harvey.
Allahabad, Futtehpoore, Cawnpoore, Banda—C. B. Thornhill.
Bareilly, Shahjehanpoore, Moradabad, Bijnore, Budaon—R. Alexander, C.B.
Benares, Mirzapore, Jaumpore, Goruckpoore, Ghazeepeore, Azimghur—F. B. Gub-
bins, C.B.
Bhaugulpore, Monghyr, Purneah, Tirhoot—G. U. Yule, C.B.
Burdwan, Hooghly (inclnding Magistracy of Howrah), Bankorah, Beerbhoom,
and Midnapore—J. H. Young.
Chittagong, Tipperah, Bulloah—G. Steer G. F. Cockburn.
Cuttack, Balasore, Pooree—G. F. Cockburn E. T. Trevor.
Dacca, Mymensing, Sylhet, Backergunge, Furreedpoore—C. T. Davidson.
Delhi, Goorgaon—J. E. L. Brandreth.
Hissar, Rohtuck, Jhujjen, and Sirsa—
Jhansi, Jaloun, Humeerpore, and Chundeyree—Capt. F. W. Pinkney, M.E.
Nuddeah, 24 Pergunnahs, including Calcutta, Baraset, Jessore, and Sunderbunds
—A. Grote.
Mcerut, Mozuffurnugger, Saharunpoore, Boolundshubur, Deyra Dhoon, Allygurh
—F. Williams.
Patna, Behar, Sarun, Shahabad, Champarun—H. D. H. Fergusson.
Rajshahye, Pubna, Rungpore, Bograh, Dinagepore, Moorshedabad, and Malda
—H. M. Reid.

ORDER OF THE BATH.

Officers of Her Majesty's Indian Forces who are Members of the Most Honourable Order of the Bath, with the dates of their appointments.

* Signifies retired from the service; B. Bengal; M. Madras; Bo. Bombay; E. Engineers; A. Artillery; C. Cavalry; I. Infantry.

MILITARY KNIGHTS GRAND CROSS.
Pollock, Gen. Sir G., B., 2 Dec. 42 Outram, Lt. Gen. Sir J., Bt., Bo., 29 July 57
Caldwell, Gen. Sir J. L., M., 30 Aug. 41 Grant, M. Gen. Sir P.
CIVIL KNIGHTS GRAND CROSS.
M'Neill,* Rt. Hon. Sir J., Bo., 15 April 39 Lawrence,* Rt. Hon. Sir J. L. M., Bt., B., 11 Nov. 57.
MILITARY KNIGHTS COMMANDERS.
Houstoun, Gen. Sir R., B., 10 Mar. 59 Hearsey, Maj. Gen. Sir J. B., B., 3 July 56
Richards, Gen. Sir W., B., 20 July 38 Wilson, Maj. Gen. Sir A., Bt., B., 14 Nov. 57.
Cheape, Lt. Gen. Sir J., B., 9 June 49 Seaton,* Col. Sir T., B., 24 Mar. 58
Steel, Maj. Gen. Sir S. W., M., 5 Dec. 53 Napier, Col. Sir R., B., 27 July 58

Regiment of Artillery.HONORARY DISTINCTIONS CONFERRED ON THE REGIMENT.HORSE ARTILLERY.

The Sphinx, with the words 'Egypt,' 'Ava,' and 'Bhurtpore.'

1st BRIGADE.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
1st Tr., 'Egypt,' 'Deig,' 'Ava,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Alliwal.'
2nd Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'
3rd Tr., 'Cabool, 1842,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'

2nd BRIGADE.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Alliwal,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
1st Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Alliwal,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
2nd Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Ava,' 'Ghuznee,' 'Afghanistan,' 'Maharajpore,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
3rd Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Cabool, 1842,'

2nd BRIGADE—continued.

'Maharajpore,' 'Alliwal,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
4th Tr., 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'

3rd BRIGADE.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
1st Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Punniar,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
2nd Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Maharajpore,' 2 guns, G. G. escort, 'Moodkee,' 2 guns, G. G. escort, 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Alliwal,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
3rd Tr., 'Punniar,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'
4th Tr., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Afghanistan,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Punjab,' 'Mooltan,' 'Goojerat.'

FOOT ARTILLERY.

A Gun, superscribed with 'Bengal Artillery,' surrounded by a Laurel Wreath and surmounted by a Crown; the word 'Ubique' below.

1st BATTALION.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Bhurtpore.'
1st Co., 'Seringapatam,' 'Maharajpore,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
2nd Co., 'Carnatic,' 'Allighur,' 'Delhi,' 'Laswarrie,' 'Deig,' 'Bhurtpore.'
3rd Co., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
4th Co., 'Seringapatam,' 'Bhurtpore.'

2nd BATTALION.

2nd Co., 'Carnatic,' 'Mysore,' 'Seringapatam,' 'Cabool, 1842,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Mooltan,' 'Goojerat.'
3rd Co., 'Arracan,' 'Cabool, 1842,' 'Ghuznee, 1842.'
4th Co., 'Arracan,' 'Afghanistan,' 'Candahar,' 'Khelat-i-Ghiltze.'

3rd BATTALION.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Bhurtpore.'
1st Co., 'Mysore,' 'Allighur,' 'Delhi,' 'Laswarrie,' 'Deig,' 'Java,' 'Bhurtpore.'
2nd Co., 'Bhurtpore.'
3rd Co., 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Mooltan,' 'Goojerat.'
4th Co., 'Java,' 'Bhurtpore,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Mooltan,' 'Goojerat.'

4th BATTALION.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Ferozeshuhur,'

4th BATTALION—continued.

'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
1st Co., 'Passey,' 'Buxar,' 'Allighur,' 'Delhi,' 'Laswarrie,' 'Deig,' 'Maharajpore,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
2nd Co., 'Carnatic,' 'Mysore,' 'Bhurtpore,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Chillianwalla,' 'Goojerat.'
3rd Co., 'Bhurtpore,' 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon.'
4th Co., 'Arracan,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab,' 'Det. Chillianwalla,' 'Det. Goojerat.'

5th BATTALION.

1st Co., 'Punjab.'
2nd Co., 'Seringapatam,' 'Allighur,' 'Delhi,' 'Laswarrie,' 'Deig,' 'Pegu.'
3rd Co., 'Ava,' 'Pegu.'
4th Co., 'Ava.'

6th BATTALION.

Hd.-Qrs., 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'
1st Co., 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'
2nd Co., 'Moodkee,' 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon.'
3rd Co., 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'
4th Co., 'Ferozeshuhur,' 'Subraon,' 'Punjab.'

IRREGULAR REGIMENTS.

Umballah.
Arr. Apr. 60.] Regt. of Kelat-i-Ghilzie, Nat. Inf.

“Kelat-i-Ghilzie”—“Candahar”—“Ghaznee”—“Cabool, 1842.”
Flank Companies.—“Maharajpoor.”

Commandant—Major H. B. Stevens, 41 N.I., 22 April 58
Second in command—Lieutenant W. Winson, 45 N.I., 13 Feb. 60
Adjutant—Lieutenant J. C. Miller, 52 N.I., 13 Feb. 60
Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon A. Christison, M.D., 18 July 60

Allypore.] Regiment of Allypore.

Commandant—Major C. Herbert, 18 N.I.
Second in command—Lieutenant F. Allen, 42 N.I., 13 Oct. 59
Adjutant—Lieutenant A. R. Loughman, 13 N.I., 18 Feb. 60
Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon

SEIKH BATTALIONS.

Serv. China.] Regiment of Loodianah.

Facings green.

Commandant—Major C. J. Walsh, 14 N.I., 18 Feb. 58
Second in command—Lieutenant R. W. Glasse, 14 N.I., 1 May 58
Adjutant—Lieutenant R. F. H. M. Skinner, 82 Foot
Quarter-Master—Captain J. Ruggles, 41 N.I., 5 May 60
Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon J. Reid, 4 July 60

Sealkote.] Regiment of Ferozepore.

Facings yellow.

Commandant—Lieutenant-Colonel J. Brasyer, C.B., Unattached, 9 Mar. 55
Second in command—Captain A. W. Montagu, 68 N.I., 9 Mar. 55
Adjutant—Lieutenant W. G. Trevor, 80 Foot, 26 Mar. 60
Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon T. S. Veale, 20 July 59

Serv. China.] Regiment of Lucknow.

Commandant—Colonel H. Palmer, 48 N.I., 13 Jan. 59
Second in command—Lieutenant N. R. Sneyd, 57 N.I., 5 May 60
Adjutant—Lieutenant Hon. H. H. Hare, 17 N.I., 17 Feb. 60
Medical charge—Surgeon J. M. Fleming, M.D., 21 Feb. 60

IRREGULAR CAVALRY.

Jullundur.
Arr. May 58. ]

1st Regiment.

"Bhurtpore"—Detachment. "Candahar, 1842."

Commandant—Lieutenant-Colonel C. T. Chamberlain, 28 N.I., 19 Nov. 49

Second in command—Lieutenant G. C. Thomson, 51 N.I., 8 Feb. 59

Adjutant—Lieutenant G. W. Cockburn, 42 Highlanders, 2 May 60

Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon E. J. Vivian, 9 June 60

Peshawur. ]

2nd Regiment.

"Arracan"—"Sobraon"—"Punjab."

Commandant—Major G. Jackson, 3 Cav., 24 Feb. 48

Second in command—Lieutenant C. Shaw, 57 N.I., 18 Feb. 60

Adjutant—Lieutenant R. W. Cox, 64 Foot, 2 April 60

Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon T. Atchison, 16 Aug. 60

Shajehanpore. ]

3rd Regiment.

"Cabool, 1842"—"Ferozeshahur"—"Punjab"—"Chillianwalla"—
"Goojerat."

Commandant—Captain R. G. Mayne, 59 N.I., 25 Sept. 58

Second in command—Lieutenant A. Cory, 16 N.I., 15 Jan. 59

Adjutant—

Medical charge—Assistant Surgeon A. Taylor, 8 June 60

Nowgong.
Arr. 9 Dec. 59. ]

4th Regiment.

"Afghanistan"—"Ghuznee"—"Maharajpore." 2 Rissalaks, "Kelat."

Regiment of Artillery.

"Seringapatam"—"Egypt"—"Assaye"—"Bourbon"—"Java"—"Seetabuldee"—
"Mahidpoor"—"Nagpoor"—"Corygaum"—"Ava."

C Troop Horse Artillery, B and C Companies, second, and D Company third Battalion Artillery bear on their Appointments "A Dragon" wearing an Imperial Crown, with the word "China."

Horse Artillery ..... Bangalore. Arrived 23rd Oct. 38
1st Battalion ..... Mount. Do. 13th Apr. 57
2nd Do. .... Kamptee. Do. 30th Jan. 57
3rd Do. .... Rangoon. Do. 6th May 57
4th Do. .... Secunderabad. Do. 6th Oct. 53
5th Do. .... Mount. Since formation (1819).

One Brigade of Horse Artillery, consisting of Four European and two Native Troops; four Battalions of European Foot Artillery, four Companies each; one Battalion of Native Foot Artillery of six Companies.

Blue; Facings Red, Lace Gold.

"Seringapatam"—"Java"—"Egypt"—"Assaye"—"Mahidpore"—"Nagpore"—
"Ava."

A, B, C, and E, Companies, "Pegu."

, B, and F Companies bear, on their appointments, a Dragon, wearing an Imperial Crown, with the word "China."

C Company bears on its appointments "Meance" and "Hyderabad, 1843."

GOVERNOR.

TOOK HIS SEAT 11TH MAY, 1860.

COUNCIL.

Member, Lieut.-Gen. Sir William Mansfield, K.C.B., Commander-in-Chief, took his seat 29th June, 1860.

Member, Henry Wilson Reeves, took his seat 15th Aug., 1857.

Member, William Edward Frere, took his seat 7th April, 1860.

Regiment of Artillery.

Cloth Blue, Facings Red, Lace Gold.

HORSE BRIGADE.—“Beri-boo-Ali”—“Ghuznee”—“Khelat” and “Afghanistan.”
1ST, OR LESLIE’S TROOP.—“Hyderabad.”
3RD TROOP.—“Punjab”—“Mooltan”—“Goojerat”—“Persia”—“Reshire”—
“Khooshab.”
1ST BATTALION.—“Seringapatam”—“Egypt” and “Assaye.”
2ND COMPANY.—“Meeanee”—“Hyderabad”—“Punjab”—“Mooltan”—“Goojerat.”
3RD COMPANY.—“Candahar, 1842.”
2ND BATTALION.—“Seringapatam”—“Kirkee”—“Ghuznee”—“Afghanistan.”
2ND COMPANY.—“Meeanee”—“Hyderabad.”
4TH COMPANY.—“Punjab”—“Aden”—“Mooltan.”
3RD BATTALION, 3RD COMPANY.—“Ghuznee”—“Afghanistan”—“Aden”—“Meeanee”—
“Hyderabad.”
4TH BATTALION, 1ST AND 2ND COMPANIES.—“Punjab”—“Mooltan.”
Horse Brigade, Poonah—1st Battalion, Ahmednuggur—2nd Battalion, Bombay—3rd
Battalion, Ahmedabad—4th Battalion, Ahmednuggur.
Reserve Artillery, 1st Company, Shikarpore—2nd Company, Gwalior—3rd Company,
Kolapore—4th Company, Kurrachee.

One Brigade of Horse Artillery, consisting of 4 European Troops; 2 Battalions of European Foot Artillery, 4 Companies each; and 2 Battalions of Native Foot Artillery, 6 Companies.
